March — August Growing Season

My Plant Collection

A care guide for 35 plants (indoor & outdoor), tailored for the Texas climate during the warm growing months.

Bird of Paradise Citrus Tree Chinese Money Plant Golden Pothos Lucky Bamboo Fiddle Leaf Fig Monstera Orchid Majesty Palm Dragon Tree Corn Plant Hydrangea Chinese Evergreen Peace Lily Impatiens Kalanchoe Aloe Vera Florist Kalanchoe Boston Fern Cactus Herb Garden Rose Curry Leaf Mandevilla Hibiscus Dipladenia Jasmine Begonia Bougainvillea Liriope Azalea Salvia Croton Canna Lily Asiatic Lily Schefflera
☀️

Texas Growing Season: March through August

Texas summers bring intense heat (often 95–105 °F) and strong sun. Most houseplants grow actively during this period but need protection from direct afternoon sun through windows, more frequent watering as pots dry faster, and extra humidity to combat air conditioning. Move plants away from south- and west-facing windows during peak summer, or use sheer curtains to filter light.

1
Moderate
Indoor
Strelitzia reginae / S. nicolai

Bird of Paradise

A dramatic tropical plant with large, paddle-shaped leaves that fan out elegantly. Native to South Africa, it can grow 5–6 ft indoors and produces striking orange-and-blue flowers when given enough light. Its bold silhouette makes it a statement piece in any room.

💧
Water
Every 7–10 days
Water thoroughly when the top 2 inches of soil are dry. In peak Texas summer (June–Aug), check every 5–7 days. Reduce slightly in spring. Allow excess water to drain completely; never let it sit in standing water.
☀️
Sunlight
Bright indirect to direct
Thrives in 6+ hours of bright light. Can handle some direct morning sun (east-facing window). In Texas summers, shield from harsh afternoon sun through south/west windows to prevent leaf scorch.
🌡️
Temperature
65–85 °F ideal
Comfortable in typical Texas indoor temps. Keep away from AC vents that blow cold air directly on leaves. Can tolerate up to 90 °F but prefers some relief from extreme heat.
🌱
Extra Care
Feed monthly, mist weekly
Fertilize with balanced liquid fertilizer every 4 weeks from March–August. Wipe leaves with a damp cloth monthly to remove dust. Appreciates humidity — mist leaves or use a pebble tray in AC-cooled rooms.
🏴
Texas Tip
Your Bird of Paradise is on the porch — it will love the warmth from March through May. Once temps consistently hit 95 °F+, consider moving it to a shadier porch spot or indoors near a bright window. Watch for spider mites, which thrive in hot, dry Texas air.
2
Moderate
Indoor
Citrus spp.

Dwarf Citrus Tree

A compact citrus tree — likely a Meyer lemon or calamondin — staked with a bamboo support. Indoor citrus trees produce fragrant blossoms and small fruits when given enough light. They bring a touch of the orchard indoors and can fruit year-round in the right conditions.

💧
Water
Every 5–7 days
Keep soil consistently moist but not waterlogged. Stick a finger 2 inches in — water when dry at that depth. In Texas summer heat, may need watering every 3–5 days. Good drainage is critical; root rot is the #1 killer of indoor citrus.
☀️
Sunlight
Full sun — 8+ hours
Citrus is a sun-hungry plant. Place by your brightest south-facing window. In Texas, it can go outdoors on a patio from April–September for maximum light. Gradually acclimate it over a week to avoid sunburn on leaves.
🌡️
Temperature
60–90 °F
Loves Texas warmth. Can handle summer heat outdoors. Bring inside if nighttime temps drop below 50 °F (typically late October in Texas). Avoid cold AC drafts indoors.
🌱
Extra Care
Citrus fertilizer, pollinate
Use a citrus-specific fertilizer every 4–6 weeks March–August. Indoors, hand-pollinate flowers with a small brush by dabbing from flower to flower. Watch for scale insects and aphids on the undersides of leaves.
🏴
Texas Tip
Texas is excellent citrus territory. Move this tree outside to a sunny patio from April through September for the best fruit production. It appears to have buds forming — keep the soil evenly moist during flowering and fruiting, as inconsistent watering causes bud and fruit drop.
3
Easy
Indoor
Pilea peperomioides

Chinese Money Plant

Known for its distinctive round, coin-shaped leaves on delicate stems, this trendy plant has become an Instagram favorite. Originally from southern China, it produces "pups" (baby plants) freely, making it perfect for sharing with friends. Yours looks full and healthy.

💧
Water
Every 7–10 days
Allow the top inch of soil to dry between waterings. Drooping leaves signal thirst. In Texas summer with AC running, indoor air dries out soil faster — check twice a week. Water less if leaves start yellowing (overwatering sign).
☀️
Sunlight
Bright indirect light
Loves bright, indirect light — an east or north-facing window is ideal. Rotate the pot a quarter turn every week so it grows evenly (it leans toward light). Avoid direct Texas afternoon sun, which will scorch the thin leaves.
🌡️
Temperature
60–80 °F
Prefers moderate temps and doesn't like extremes. Keep away from hot windows in summer and cold AC vents. Does well in typical Texas indoor conditions year-round.
🌱
Extra Care
Light feeding, propagate pups
Feed with diluted balanced fertilizer once a month from March–July. Separate baby "pups" that sprout from the soil to share or repot. Clean leaves with a damp cloth occasionally — dust blocks light absorption on those flat coins.
🏴
Texas Tip
Your Pilea is on the kitchen counter by a window — a good spot. In summer, if that window gets harsh afternoon sun, slide it back a foot or add a sheer curtain. The dry AC air in Texas homes can curl leaf edges; a small humidifier nearby or grouping plants together helps.
4
Easy
Indoor
Epipremnum aureum

Golden Pothos

One of the most forgiving and popular houseplants on the planet. Its heart-shaped, glossy green leaves trail beautifully from shelves or hanging baskets. Nearly indestructible, it purifies air and thrives on neglect — the ideal starter plant for any home.

💧
Water
Every 7–14 days
Very drought-tolerant. Water when the top half of the soil is dry. Wilting leaves bounce back after watering. In Texas summer with AC, every 7–10 days is typical. Better to underwater than overwater — yellow leaves mean too much water.
☀️
Sunlight
Low to bright indirect
Incredibly adaptable — survives in low light but grows faster in bright indirect light. More light brings out the golden variegation. Keep out of direct sun, which bleaches and burns leaves. Can thrive even in interior rooms.
🌡️
Temperature
60–90 °F
Handles a wide temperature range easily. Happy in any Texas indoor setting. Avoid cold drafts below 50 °F and direct hot air from heating vents.
🌱
Extra Care
Trim vines, easy to propagate
Feed lightly every 6–8 weeks during growing season with balanced liquid fertilizer. Trim leggy vines to encourage bushier growth. Propagate cuttings in water — just snip below a node and place in a jar. Roots appear within 1–2 weeks.
🏴
Texas Tip
Pothos is nearly foolproof in Texas homes. Your plant looks lush and healthy. In summer, it will grow fast with the long daylight hours. Consider training the vines to climb a moss pole or trailing from a high shelf. This plant also does great in bathrooms and kitchens where humidity is naturally higher.
5
Easy
Indoor
Dracaena sanderiana

Lucky Bamboo

Despite its name, this isn't a true bamboo — it's a Dracaena native to Central Africa. Popular in feng shui, different stalk counts carry different meanings (3 = happiness, 5 = health). It can grow in water or soil and is quite low-maintenance. Yours shows some browning leaf tips, which is common and fixable.

💧
Water
Keep roots submerged / soil moist
If in water: change the water every 1–2 weeks and keep roots covered. Use filtered or distilled water — Lucky Bamboo is very sensitive to chlorine and fluoride in Texas tap water, which causes brown tips. If in soil: keep evenly moist.
☀️
Sunlight
Low to moderate indirect
Prefers filtered, indirect light. Direct sun will burn the leaves. Does well in interior rooms, offices, or north-facing windows. In Texas, keep far from south/west windows during summer.
🌡️
Temperature
65–90 °F
Comfortable in typical Texas indoor temps. Avoid placing near AC vents or drafty spots. Can handle warmth but may dry out faster in hot rooms — check water level more often in summer.
🌱
Extra Care
Filtered water is key
Trim brown tips with clean scissors at an angle. Feed very sparingly — one drop of liquid fertilizer per water change is enough. Over-fertilizing turns stalks yellow. If growing in water, rinse pebbles/stones when changing water to prevent algae.
🏴
Texas Tip
The browning leaf tips on yours are likely from chlorine/fluoride in Texas municipal water. Switch to filtered, distilled, or rainwater and you should see improvement on new growth. Texas tap water is often hard and treated heavily — Lucky Bamboo is one of the most sensitive plants to this. Trim the brown tips for a cleaner look.
6
Demanding
Indoor
Ficus lyrata

Fiddle Leaf Fig

The Instagram darling of the plant world, known for its large, violin-shaped, deeply veined leaves. Native to tropical West Africa, it's a stunning focal point — but has a reputation for being finicky. It dislikes change, drafts, and inconsistent care. Yours has beautiful, large leaves and looks well-established.

💧
Water
Every 7–10 days
Water when the top 2 inches of soil are dry. Water thoroughly until it drains from the bottom, then empty the saucer. Consistency is key — same day each week works well. In Texas summer, may need water every 5–7 days. Brown spots on leaves often signal overwatering.
☀️
Sunlight
Bright indirect light
Needs lots of bright, filtered light — at least 6 hours daily. East-facing windows are ideal. Can tolerate some direct morning sun. In Texas summer, avoid unfiltered afternoon sun through south/west windows. Rotate the pot monthly for even growth.
🌡️
Temperature
65–75 °F ideal
Dislikes temperature swings. Keep away from AC vents, exterior doors, and drafty windows. In Texas, the biggest risk is AC blasting cold air directly on the plant — this causes leaf drop. Maintain consistent temps.
🌱
Extra Care
Don't move it, high humidity
Fiddle Leaf Figs hate being moved — find a good spot and leave it. Feed with a Ficus-specific fertilizer monthly March–August. Clean leaves with a damp cloth to maximize photosynthesis. Humidity of 40–60% is ideal; use a humidifier in AC-dried rooms.
🏴
Texas Tip
The biggest threat to a Fiddle Leaf Fig in Texas is the combination of intense AC indoors and extreme heat outdoors. Never move it outside in Texas summer — the shock will cause massive leaf drop. Keep it in a consistently lit indoor spot, away from vents. If leaf edges are browning, your AC is likely pulling too much moisture from the air — add a humidifier nearby.
7
Easy
Indoor
Monstera deliciosa

Monstera (Swiss Cheese Plant)

One of the most iconic houseplants, recognized by its huge, glossy leaves with natural holes and splits (fenestrations). Native to Mexican and Central American rainforests, it's a vigorous climber. Yours is a mature specimen with impressive fenestrations — a sign of great care and good light.

💧
Water
Every 7–12 days
Water when the top 2–3 inches of soil are dry. Monsteras like to dry out a bit between waterings. In Texas summer with AC running, every 7–9 days is typical. Yellow leaves = overwatering, brown crispy edges = underwatering. Water deeply and let it drain.
☀️
Sunlight
Bright indirect light
Bright indirect light produces the most fenestrations (splits). Tolerates medium light but grows slower and produces smaller, less-split leaves. Avoid direct Texas afternoon sun — it burns the leaves. A few feet from a bright window is perfect.
🌡️
Temperature
65–85 °F
Very comfortable in Texas indoor temperatures. Grows fastest when warm. Keep away from AC vents. Can be placed outdoors in a shaded patio spot during warm months, but acclimate gradually.
🌱
Extra Care
Support, feed, and clean
Provide a moss pole or trellis — Monsteras are natural climbers and grow larger leaves with support. Feed monthly March–August with balanced fertilizer. Wipe large leaves with a damp cloth to keep them shiny and dust-free. Prune leggy growth to encourage fullness.
🏴
Texas Tip
Your Monstera is thriving — the large, well-split leaves mean it's getting good light. During the Texas growing season (March–August), this plant will push out new leaves rapidly. Each new leaf should be bigger and more fenestrated than the last. Add a moss pole if you haven't already — it will reward you with dramatically larger leaves. Watch for fungus gnats in humid Texas conditions; let the soil dry between waterings to prevent them.
35
Moderate
Indoor
Phalaenopsis spp.

Moth Orchid

The most popular orchid in the world, prized for its elegant arching flower spikes and long-lasting blooms. Your Phalaenopsis has two stunning flower spikes bearing warm copper-amber flowers with purple-pink markings — a striking color variety. Moth orchids are epiphytes (air plants) that grow on tree branches in the wild, which means their care is quite different from typical houseplants. With proper watering and light, they can rebloom for months every year.

💧
Watering
Weekly, ice cube method works
Water once a week by soaking the roots for 10–15 minutes in room-temperature water, then drain completely. Alternatively, place 3 ice cubes on the bark mix weekly — they melt slowly and prevent overwatering. Never let the roots sit in standing water, as this causes root rot. The roots should be silvery-green when dry and bright green after watering.
☀️
Light
Bright indirect — east or north window
Phalaenopsis thrive in bright, indirect light. An east-facing window is ideal — gentle morning sun without harsh afternoon rays. North-facing windows also work well. Avoid direct south or west sun, which will burn the leaves (look for yellow or brown patches). If the leaves are dark green, the orchid needs more light; medium olive-green means the light level is perfect.
🌡️
Temperature
65–80 °F, needs a cool drop to bloom
Comfortable at normal room temperatures (65–80 °F). To trigger reblooming, the orchid needs a 10–15 °F temperature drop at night for about 2–4 weeks in fall — placing it near a slightly cool window in autumn does the trick. Avoid cold drafts below 55 °F and keep away from heating vents, which dry them out quickly.
🌱
Extra Care
Reblooming, humidity, and feeding
After all flowers drop, cut the spike above the second node from the base — it may branch and rebloom. If the spike turns brown, cut it to the base; a new spike will grow in a few months. Feed every 2 weeks with orchid fertilizer (20-20-20 diluted to quarter strength) during active growth. Orchids love 50–70% humidity; set on a pebble tray with water or group with other plants. Repot every 1–2 years in fresh orchid bark mix when roots outgrow the pot.
🏴
Texas Tip
Phalaenopsis orchids do well indoors in Texas year-round. The biggest challenge is the dry air from air conditioning in summer and heating in winter — both drop humidity below what orchids prefer. Use a pebble tray or a small humidifier nearby. Your window placement looks great for light. To trigger reblooming in fall, take advantage of the natural temperature drop — place it near a window where nighttime temps dip to the mid-60s for a few weeks in October or November. Texas tap water is often hard and alkaline; using filtered or rainwater prevents mineral buildup on roots. Your copper-amber blooms are gorgeous and can last 2–3 months with proper care.
36
Moderate
Indoor
Ravenea rivularis

Majesty Palm

A graceful tropical palm native to Madagascar, prized for its elegant arching fronds that fan out symmetrically from a central trunk. Majesty Palms can reach 10 ft indoors and bring an instant resort-like atmosphere to any room or covered patio. They thrive in humidity and warmth, making them well-suited to Austin's muggy summers — though they do need attention to watering and light to stay lush.

💧
Water
Every 5–7 days, keep moist
Majesty Palms prefer consistently moist (not soggy) soil — they grow along riverbanks in the wild. Water when the top inch of soil feels dry. In Austin's peak summer heat, you may need to water every 4–5 days. Reduce to every 10–14 days in winter. Use filtered or rainwater if possible — Austin's hard, alkaline tap water can cause browning leaf tips over time.
☀️
Sunlight
Bright indirect, no harsh afternoon sun
Thrives in bright, indirect light — 6+ hours daily is ideal. An east-facing window or covered porch with filtered light works perfectly. Can tolerate some gentle morning direct sun, but harsh afternoon sun (especially through south or west windows in Texas) will scorch the fronds. If fronds turn pale yellow-green, the plant is getting too much direct sun; if they stretch and droop, it needs more light.
🌡️
Temperature
65–85 °F, bring in below 45 °F
Loves warmth and handles Austin's hot summers well. Keep above 45 °F — anything near freezing will cause serious damage. Outdoors on a covered porch from March through November is ideal. Bring indoors when overnight lows approach 45 °F, typically late November. Avoid placing near AC vents or drafts indoors, which dry out the fronds.
🌱
Extra Care
Humidity, acidic fertilizer, misting
Majesty Palms are humidity lovers — aim for 50%+ humidity. Mist fronds a few times a week or use a pebble tray indoors, especially when AC or heating is running. Feed monthly from March through September with an acidic palm fertilizer (or add a tablespoon of Epsom salt per gallon of water monthly to prevent magnesium deficiency, which causes yellowing fronds). Trim brown-tipped fronds at the base — some lower frond loss is normal as the palm grows.
🏴
Texas Tip
Your covered porch setup is perfect for this palm — it gets warmth and humidity without direct scorching sun. Keep it potted so you can bring it inside when Austin gets its occasional winter freezes (remember 2021!). The biggest indoor challenge is dry air from AC in summer; a humidifier or regular misting keeps fronds from browning. Austin's alkaline tap water is this palm's enemy — it prefers slightly acidic soil (pH 5.0–6.0). Using filtered water or mixing in a bit of vinegar (1 tsp per gallon) helps. Watch for spider mites in hot, dry conditions — a weekly shower spray on the fronds keeps them at bay.
8
Easy
Outdoor
Dracaena marginata

Dragon Tree

An elegant, sculptural tropical plant with thin, sword-like leaves growing from tall, narrow, branching woody canes. Native to Madagascar, the Dragon Tree is a low-maintenance statement plant with a dramatic architectural silhouette. Your plant has developed beautiful branching canes, each topped with a spray of narrow, pendulous leaves.

💧
Watering
Low — drought tolerant
Allow the top half of the soil to dry out between waterings. Dracaena marginata is more likely to suffer from overwatering than underwatering. Water every 1–2 weeks in summer, less in winter. Ensure excellent drainage — soggy soil causes root rot quickly. Sensitive to fluoride in tap water.
☀️
Light
Bright indirect light
Prefers bright filtered light and tolerates lower light conditions. On the porch, the covered/shaded position is ideal. Direct hot afternoon sun can scorch the narrow leaves. Some morning sun is fine and helps maintain the reddish leaf edges.
🌡️
Temperature
65–80 °F ideal
Tropical plant that loves warmth. Thrives on a sheltered porch during warm Texas months. Not frost-hardy at all — bring indoors when temperatures drop below 50 °F. Handles indoor conditions well in winter as a houseplant.
🌱
Extra Care
Prune for shape, minimal feeding
Dragon Trees need very little fuss. Prune canes at any height to encourage branching — new shoots will sprout below the cut. Feed lightly once a month in the growing season with a balanced liquid fertilizer. Wipe the narrow leaves occasionally to remove dust. Lower leaves naturally yellow and drop as the plant grows taller.
🏴
Texas Tip
Your Dragon Tree has a beautiful branching form on the porch. The covered position is perfect — it filters the intense Texas sun that would scorch the narrow leaves. These plants are sensitive to the fluoride and minerals in Texas tap water; switch to filtered or rainwater if you notice brown leaf tips. Bring indoors before the first cold snap and place near a bright window. It adapts well to indoor conditions over winter.
9
Easy
Outdoor
Dracaena fragrans

Corn Plant

A tall, tree-like tropical plant with thick woody canes and long, arching, strap-shaped leaves. Native to tropical Africa, the Corn Plant gets its common name from leaves that resemble corn stalks. It's a classic statement plant that adds a tropical feel to any porch or living space.

💧
Watering
Low to moderate
Allow the top half of the soil to dry out between waterings. Dracaenas are drought-tolerant and are more likely to suffer from overwatering than underwatering. Water thoroughly when you do water, and ensure good drainage. Reduce frequency in winter.
☀️
Light
Bright indirect, tolerates low light
Prefers bright filtered light but is famously tolerant of lower light conditions. On the porch, keep it out of direct afternoon sun, which can bleach or burn the leaves. Morning sun is fine.
🌡️
Temperature
60–80 °F ideal
Tropical plant that does not tolerate cold. Bring indoors when temperatures drop below 55 °F. Thrives in the warmth of a Texas porch during spring and summer months.
🌱
Extra Care
Wipe leaves, watch for pests
Dust accumulates on the large leaves — wipe them with a damp cloth regularly. Feed monthly in spring and summer with a diluted balanced fertilizer. Dracaenas are sensitive to fluoride; use distilled or filtered water if you notice brown leaf tips. Prune canes to control height.
🏴
Texas Tip
Your Corn Plant looks well-established on the porch. It will love the warmth and humidity of Texas spring. Move it indoors before temperatures drop below 55 °F in fall. These plants are sensitive to the chemicals in Texas tap water — if you notice browning leaf tips, switch to filtered or rainwater. The covered porch provides good filtered light for this plant.
10
Moderate
Outdoor
Hydrangea macrophylla

Hydrangea

A beloved flowering shrub producing large, globe-shaped flower clusters in shades of pink, blue, and purple. The flower color depends on soil pH — acidic soil produces blue flowers, while alkaline soil yields pink. Your potted hydrangea is blooming with lovely pink flower heads on the porch.

💧
Watering
Consistent moisture, never dry out
Hydrangeas are heavy drinkers — the name literally means 'water vessel.' Water deeply and regularly, especially during hot weather. In containers, you may need to water daily in Texas summers. Wilting leaves are the first sign of thirst. Mulch the soil surface to retain moisture.
☀️
Light
Morning sun, afternoon shade
Best with 3–4 hours of gentle morning sun followed by afternoon shade. Too much direct sun in Texas will scorch the leaves and fade the blooms. The porch provides good filtered light.
🌡️
Temperature
60–75 °F for blooming
Hydrangeas prefer moderate temperatures. Extreme Texas heat (above 90 °F) can stress them — move to the shadiest porch spot during heat waves. They need winter chill for next year's blooms, so leave them outdoors until frost threatens, then move to a cool garage.
🌱
Extra Care
Prune after blooming, adjust pH
Prune immediately after flowering — hydrangeas bloom on old wood, so late pruning removes next year's buds. To keep pink flowers, add garden lime to the soil. For blue flowers, add aluminum sulfate. Feed in spring with a slow-release fertilizer for acid-loving plants.
🏴
Texas Tip
Texas soil tends to be alkaline, which is why your hydrangea is blooming pink. If you want blue flowers, amend the potting soil with aluminum sulfate and use acidic fertilizer. In Texas heat, potted hydrangeas need water almost daily — check every morning. Move to the shadiest spot on the porch when temperatures hit the mid-90s. The potted setup is smart because you can move it as needed.
11
Easy
Outdoor
Aglaonema commutatum

Chinese Evergreen

A stunning tropical foliage plant with large, oval leaves splashed with vibrant pink, red, and green variegation. Chinese Evergreens are prized for their colorful leaves and low-maintenance nature. Your specimen shows beautiful pink veining against green — a newer cultivar bred for intense color.

💧
Watering
Low to moderate
Allow the top inch or two of soil to dry between waterings. Aglaonemas prefer slightly moist soil but are forgiving of occasional neglect. Overwatering is the most common killer — ensure good drainage and never let the pot sit in standing water.
☀️
Light
Bright indirect to low light
Very tolerant of low light conditions. The colorful varieties like yours need brighter indirect light to maintain their vivid pink and red coloring. Avoid direct sun, which will bleach and burn the leaves.
🌡️
Temperature
65–80 °F ideal
Tropical plant that hates cold. Bring indoors when temperatures drop below 60 °F. Does well on a sheltered porch during Texas warm months. Avoid cold drafts and sudden temperature changes.
🌱
Extra Care
Clean leaves, feed lightly
Wipe the large decorative leaves with a damp cloth to keep them glossy and free of dust. Feed monthly in spring and summer with a balanced liquid fertilizer diluted to half strength. Remove any yellow lower leaves as the plant matures — this is normal.
🏴
Texas Tip
Your Aglaonema has gorgeous coloring — the pink variegation is eye-catching. On the porch, make sure it stays in the shade. Even bright indirect outdoor light is much stronger than indoor light, and these leaves can burn quickly in Texas sun. This plant does well with the humidity of a Texas porch but bring it inside well before the first cold front. Watch for mealybugs in the warm, humid conditions.
12
Easy
Outdoor
Spathiphyllum wallisii

Peace Lily

A lush, tropical plant with large, dark green, glossy leaves that arch gracefully. Peace Lilies are famous for their elegant white spathes (modified leaves that look like flowers) and their ability to thrive in low light. They're also top-rated air purifiers. Your specimen is impressively large and full.

💧
Watering
Keep evenly moist
Water when the top inch of soil feels dry. Peace Lilies will dramatically wilt when thirsty — they're communicative plants — but bounce back quickly after watering. Don't let them sit in water. They prefer consistent moisture without being waterlogged.
☀️
Light
Low to medium indirect light
One of the best low-light plants available. Too much direct sun will scorch the leaves. On the porch, keep in a shaded spot — the filtered light of a covered porch is ideal. More light encourages flowering.
🌡️
Temperature
65–85 °F ideal
Tropical origins mean it loves warmth and humidity. Perfect on a Texas porch during the warm months. Bring inside when temperatures dip below 55 °F. Avoid cold drafts.
🌱
Extra Care
Humidity, fertilize sparingly
Mist occasionally or group with other plants for humidity. Feed monthly in spring and summer with a diluted balanced fertilizer. Brown leaf tips usually indicate dry air or mineral buildup from tap water. Wipe large leaves to remove dust. Remove spent flower stalks at the base.
🏴
Texas Tip
Your Peace Lily is impressively large and looks very healthy on the porch. These plants are sensitive to the minerals in Texas tap water — if you see brown leaf tips, switch to filtered water. The covered porch provides the perfect light level. During Texas heat waves, you may need to water more frequently as the pot dries faster outdoors. Bring it inside before any cold front below 55 °F.
13
Easy
Outdoor
Impatiens walleriana

Impatiens

A classic shade-loving annual covered in cheerful, bright red-orange flowers. Impatiens are workhorses of the shade garden, blooming continuously from spring through fall without deadheading. Your plant is loaded with vibrant red blooms, creating a gorgeous splash of color on the porch.

💧
Watering
Consistent moisture
Keep soil evenly moist — impatiens wilt dramatically when dry but recover quickly with water. In containers on a hot Texas porch, expect to water daily or even twice daily in peak summer. Mulch the soil surface to help retain moisture.
☀️
Light
Partial to full shade
Impatiens are one of the few flowering plants that thrive in shade. They prefer morning sun with afternoon shade, or full dappled shade. Direct hot sun will stress them and reduce flowering. Your porch location is ideal.
🌡️
Temperature
60–85 °F for best blooming
Perform best in moderate temperatures. Extreme Texas heat above 90 °F can cause them to look tired and stop blooming temporarily. They'll bounce back when temperatures moderate. Frost kills them instantly.
🌱
Extra Care
Deadhead optional, feed regularly
One of the few flowers that doesn't need deadheading — spent blooms drop naturally and new ones follow. Feed every 2 weeks with a liquid flower fertilizer for non-stop blooms. Pinch leggy stems to encourage bushier growth. Watch for downy mildew in humid conditions.
🏴
Texas Tip
Impatiens are perfect for a shaded Texas porch — they give you non-stop color where most flowers can't grow. During the intense July–August heat, they may slow down; keep them well-watered and they'll rebound. In Texas, these are grown as annuals and won't survive winter. Consider taking cuttings in fall to root indoors for next year. Watch for spider mites in hot, dry conditions.
14
Easy
Outdoor
Kalanchoe blossfeldiana

Kalanchoe

A popular succulent known for its clusters of long-lasting, vibrant pink double flowers above thick, scalloped dark green leaves. Originally from Madagascar, Kalanchoe is one of the most popular flowering succulents, blooming for weeks at a time. Your plant is covered in gorgeous hot pink blooms.

💧
Watering
Low — let dry between waterings
As a succulent, Kalanchoe stores water in its thick leaves and prefers to dry out between waterings. Water thoroughly when the top 2 inches of soil are dry, then let it dry again. Overwatering causes root rot. Water less in winter.
☀️
Light
Bright indirect to direct morning sun
Loves bright light. Some direct morning sun is beneficial, but protect from intense afternoon sun. On the porch, a spot with morning sun and afternoon shade is perfect. More light means more flower production.
🌡️
Temperature
60–85 °F ideal
Handles Texas warmth well as long as it's not in standing water. Bring indoors when temperatures drop below 50 °F. Not frost-hardy at all.
🌱
Extra Care
Force reblooming with darkness
After blooming, remove spent flower stalks. To trigger reblooming, give the plant 14 hours of complete darkness per day for 6 weeks (cover with a box or move to a dark closet). Feed monthly during the growing season with a balanced fertilizer diluted to half strength.
🏴
Texas Tip
Kalanchoe thrives on a Texas porch during the warm months. Its succulent nature means it handles the heat better than most flowering plants — just don't overwater. The covered porch protects it from afternoon sun and rain, both of which can damage it. To get it to rebloom after the current flowers fade, cut back the spent stalks and give it a period of long nights (14 hours dark) for about 6 weeks.
15
Easy
Outdoor
Aloe barbadensis miller

Aloe Vera

The quintessential medicinal succulent, with thick, fleshy, lance-shaped leaves filled with a soothing gel used for burns, skin care, and more. Native to the Arabian Peninsula, Aloe Vera is incredibly low-maintenance and rewarding. Your plant looks healthy in its white wooden planter on the porch.

💧
Watering
Very low — drought tolerant
Water deeply but infrequently. Let the soil dry out completely between waterings — typically every 2–3 weeks. In winter, water even less (once a month or so). Overwatering is the #1 killer of aloe. Ensure excellent drainage.
☀️
Light
Bright indirect to direct sun
Loves bright light and can handle some direct sun, especially morning sun. Gradually acclimate to direct sun to avoid sunburn (leaves turn reddish-brown). The porch with filtered light is a good transition spot.
🌡️
Temperature
65–80 °F ideal
Handles heat well but is not frost-hardy. Bring indoors when temperatures drop below 50 °F. Aloe can handle the Texas heat as long as it has some afternoon shade and isn't overwatered.
🌱
Extra Care
Harvest gel, propagate pups
Break off a lower leaf and split it open to access the medicinal gel for burns and skin care. Aloe produces offsets (pups) around the base — separate and repot these for new plants. Use fast-draining cactus/succulent soil. Feed sparingly, if at all — once or twice during the growing season is plenty.
🏴
Texas Tip
Aloe Vera does well on a Texas porch during the warm season. It loves the heat but watch out for intense afternoon sun, which can sunburn the leaves (they'll turn red/brown). The white planter box is a nice touch — just make sure it has drainage holes. Texas rain can be a problem if the pot fills with water; keep it under the covered section. Use the gel for sunburns — very practical in Texas summers!
16
Easy
Outdoor
Kalanchoe blossfeldiana

Florist Kalanchoe

Another variety of the popular flowering succulent, this one producing delicate clusters of tubular orange-red flowers. Paired here with a fern on the porch, it adds warm color to the shade. The scalloped succulent leaves are thick and waxy, storing water for drought tolerance.

💧
Watering
Low — let dry between waterings
Water only when the soil is dry to the touch. As a succulent, it's far more tolerant of underwatering than overwatering. In a terra cotta pot like yours, the soil dries faster, so check weekly. Reduce watering significantly in winter.
☀️
Light
Bright indirect light
Prefers bright filtered light. Some morning sun is great, but avoid hot afternoon direct sun. The porch provides good conditions — enough light for flowering without the intensity that causes leaf burn.
🌡️
Temperature
60–85 °F ideal
Comfortable in Texas warmth during the growing season. Not cold-hardy — bring inside well before the first frost when temperatures drop below 50 °F.
🌱
Extra Care
Deadhead for continuous blooms
Remove spent flower clusters by cutting the stalk down to where the leaves begin. This redirects energy into producing new blooms. Feed every 2 weeks during active blooming with a half-strength balanced fertilizer. The terra cotta pot provides good breathability for the roots.
🏴
Texas Tip
This Kalanchoe pairs nicely with the fern on your porch — they have complementary care needs in terms of light. The terra cotta pot is a good choice because it prevents overwatering by wicking moisture. During Texas summers, the pot may dry out fast in the heat, so check it weekly. Bring indoors before the first cold snap. These make great windowsill plants during winter.
17
Moderate
Outdoor
Nephrolepis exaltata

Boston Fern

A lush, graceful fern with long, arching fronds covered in small, bright green leaflets. Boston Ferns are classic porch plants across the Southern United States, creating a cascade of green that's both elegant and refreshing. Your fern looks full and vibrant with tightly packed fronds.

💧
Watering
Keep consistently moist
Boston Ferns love moisture — never let the soil dry out completely. Water when the top of the soil starts to feel dry. In hot Texas weather, this might mean daily watering. Mist the fronds regularly to boost humidity. Don't let the pot sit in standing water though.
☀️
Light
Bright indirect light, no direct sun
Thrives in the filtered light of a covered porch — this is the classic Boston Fern environment. Direct sun will scorch and yellow the delicate fronds. Dappled shade or bright indirect light is perfect.
🌡️
Temperature
60–75 °F ideal
Prefers moderate temperatures and high humidity. Texas heat above 95 °F can stress them, but consistent watering helps. Bring indoors when temperatures drop below 50 °F in fall.
🌱
Extra Care
Humidity is key, feed monthly
Mist frequently or set on a pebble tray with water. Remove brown or dead fronds from the base to keep it looking tidy and promote new growth. Feed monthly during the growing season with a diluted balanced fertilizer. These ferns can get quite large — divide in spring if it outgrows its pot.
🏴
Texas Tip
Boston Ferns are THE classic Texas porch plant — yours is in its ideal home. The covered porch provides the perfect combination of filtered light, warmth, and some humidity. In peak Texas summer heat, you may need to water daily and mist the fronds. If fronds start dropping tiny leaflets everywhere, the air is too dry or the plant needs more water. Bring inside before the first frost and place in a bright bathroom for best results.
18
Easy
Outdoor
Echinopsis / Mammillaria spp.

Cactus Collection

A charming collection of small cacti in a terracotta pot, featuring what appears to be a columnar Echinopsis (torch cactus) alongside a round Mammillaria (pincushion cactus). These desert natives are perfectly adapted to heat and drought, storing water in their thick stems.

💧
Watering
Very low — extremely drought tolerant
Water sparingly — soak the soil thoroughly, then let it dry out completely before watering again. In summer, this might be every 1–2 weeks. In winter, once a month or less. Overwatering is the most common cause of cactus death. Ensure the pot has excellent drainage.
☀️
Light
Full sun to bright indirect
Cacti love sun. They can handle full direct sun, including Texas afternoon sun, once acclimated. Gradually increase sun exposure to avoid sunburn. The porch rail gets good light exposure.
🌡️
Temperature
50–100 °F tolerant
Heat-loving plants that handle Texas summers with ease. Most can tolerate brief cold snaps down to about 40 °F, but prolonged freezing will kill them. Bring indoors for winter.
🌱
Extra Care
Fast-draining soil, minimal feeding
Use a cactus/succulent mix with added perlite for excellent drainage. Feed once or twice during the growing season with a cactus-specific fertilizer. Don't fertilize in winter. Handle with thick gloves or wrap in newspaper when repotting. Some varieties may bloom with enough sun.
🏴
Texas Tip
Cacti are naturals for Texas — they love the heat and sun. Your porch rail spot gets good light. The main risk in Texas is actually too much rain rather than too little sun. Keep them under the covered section of the porch so they don't get waterlogged from summer thunderstorms. In winter, bring them inside to a sunny windowsill. With enough sun, the Echinopsis may reward you with spectacular large flowers.
19
Easy
Outdoor
Mentha spp. / Mixed herbs

Herb Garden

A large terra cotta pot overflowing with a mix of herbs, primarily mint with its characteristic crinkled, aromatic leaves. You've also got what appears to be oxalis (shamrock plant) and possibly some other herbs mixed in. Mint is vigorous and aromatic — perfect for cooking and drinks.

💧
Watering
Keep consistently moist
Mint is a thirsty plant — keep the soil evenly moist but not waterlogged. In a large terra cotta pot in Texas heat, check daily and water when the top feels dry. Terra cotta dries faster than plastic, which mint actually appreciates because it prevents root rot.
☀️
Light
Partial shade to partial sun
Mint prefers some sun but tolerates shade well. 4–6 hours of morning sun with afternoon shade is ideal. In Texas, too much hot afternoon sun can wilt and scorch the leaves.
🌡️
Temperature
55–85 °F for best growth
Mint is quite hardy and can handle a range of temperatures. Growth slows in extreme heat but rebounds in fall. Most mint varieties can survive mild Texas winters outdoors, dying back and returning in spring.
🌱
Extra Care
Harvest often, contain its spread
Harvest frequently by pinching stems — this encourages bushy, compact growth. Keeping mint in a pot is wise, as it's extremely invasive in the ground. Cut back by half if it gets leggy. Feed monthly with a balanced fertilizer. The leaves are wonderful fresh in tea, mojitos, salads, and as a cooking herb.
🏴
Texas Tip
Smart move keeping the mint in a pot — it would take over your entire garden bed in a Texas growing season. Harvest it often; the more you pick, the bushier it grows. Texas summers can be tough on mint when temperatures exceed 95 °F — move it to a shadier spot and keep it well-watered during heat waves. Mint is one of the few herbs that actually prefers some shade in Texas. Great for fresh mint iced tea!
20
Moderate
Outdoor
Rosa spp.

Rose Bush

A potted rose bush with soft white-to-blush-pink blooms amid dark green foliage. Roses are the classic garden flower, beloved for centuries for their beauty and fragrance. Your container rose is blooming nicely on the porch with several open flowers and buds forming.

💧
Watering
Deep, regular watering
Water deeply 2–3 times per week, more often in peak summer heat. Roses prefer deep soaking over frequent light watering to encourage deep root growth. Water at the base, not on the leaves, to prevent fungal diseases. Ensure the pot drains well.
☀️
Light
Full sun (6+ hours)
Roses need at least 6 hours of direct sun for best blooming. Morning sun is particularly important as it dries dew from leaves, preventing disease. Your porch may need to be supplemented by moving it to a sunnier spot.
🌡️
Temperature
60–85 °F for best blooming
Roses bloom best in moderate temperatures. Texas heat above 95 °F can cause bloom pause — roses may stop flowering and go semi-dormant in the hottest weeks, then resume in fall. This is normal.
🌱
Extra Care
Prune, feed, and watch for pests
Deadhead spent blooms to encourage repeat flowering — cut just above a five-leaflet leaf. Feed every 2 weeks during the growing season with a rose-specific fertilizer. Watch for aphids, black spot, and powdery mildew. Prune heavily in late February in Texas to shape and remove dead wood.
🏴
Texas Tip
Roses in Texas have two peak blooming seasons — spring (March–May) and fall (October–November). They often take a rest during the brutal July–August heat, which is normal. Your potted rose may need more sun than the porch provides — consider moving it to the sunniest spot available. The biggest challenges in Texas are black spot fungus (water at the base, not overhead) and spider mites in hot weather. Feed every 2 weeks for the best blooms.
21
Moderate
Outdoor
Murraya koenigii

Curry Leaf Plant

An aromatic tropical tree prized in South Asian cooking for its intensely fragrant compound leaves. Each leaf is made up of many small, glossy leaflets along a central stem. The fresh leaves release a distinctive warm, nutty, curry-like aroma when crushed — essential in South Indian, Sri Lankan, and Southeast Asian cuisines. Your plant shows healthy bright green growth.

💧
Watering
Moderate, consistent moisture
Keep soil evenly moist during the growing season. Water when the top inch of soil feels dry. Good drainage is essential — curry leaf plants dislike waterlogged soil. Reduce watering in winter when growth slows. In a terra cotta pot, check soil moisture frequently in Texas heat as it dries faster.
☀️
Light
Full sun to bright indirect
Curry leaf plants love sun — at least 6 hours of direct sunlight produces the most aromatic, flavorful leaves. The more sun it gets, the bushier and more productive it becomes. Can tolerate partial shade but will grow leggy. Acclimate gradually when moving outdoors in spring.
🌡️
Temperature
60–95 °F — loves heat
Curry leaf plants are tropical and thrive in hot weather — perfect for Texas summers. Growth accelerates when temperatures are consistently above 80 °F. Not frost-hardy at all; bring indoors when temps drop below 50 °F. Overwinter in a bright south-facing window.
🌱
Extra Care
Harvest often, feed for growth
Harvest leaves regularly by picking whole sprigs — this encourages bushier, more productive growth. Use leaves fresh for the best flavor; they lose potency when dried. Feed monthly during the growing season with a balanced fertilizer or iron-rich supplement. Prune to control shape. Can be propagated from fresh seeds (berries) or root suckers.
🏴
Texas Tip
Curry leaf plants absolutely love Texas summers — the heat and intense sun boost the essential oil production in the leaves, making them extra aromatic and flavorful. Keep it in the sunniest porch spot during the growing season. Harvest leaves right before cooking for the best flavor — they're essential for tempering (tadka) in South Indian dishes, and wonderful in curries, chutneys, and soups. Must come indoors for winter; place near the sunniest window. Growth will slow indoors but the plant will survive to thrive again next summer.
22
Moderate
Outdoor
Mandevilla sanderi

Mandevilla

A stunning tropical vine producing large, trumpet-shaped red flowers against glossy dark green leaves. Mandevilla is a show-stopping climber that blooms continuously from spring through fall. Your vine on the porch has beautiful vibrant red blooms — a classic porch plant in the South.

💧
Watering
Moderate, let top dry slightly
Water when the top inch of soil dries out. Mandevilla prefers evenly moist but not soggy soil. In containers, water thoroughly until it drains from the bottom. Reduce watering in winter when growth slows.
☀️
Light
Full sun to bright indirect
Blooms best with at least 6 hours of sunlight. Morning sun with some afternoon shade works well in Texas. The more sun it gets, the more flowers it produces. Can tolerate partial shade but will flower less.
🌡️
Temperature
60–90 °F for active growth
A true tropical that loves warmth. Thrives during Texas summers. Not cold-hardy at all — bring indoors or protect when temperatures drop below 50 °F. Can be overwintered indoors as a houseplant.
🌱
Extra Care
Support for climbing, feed for blooms
Provide a trellis, stake, or railing for the vine to climb. Feed every 2 weeks during the blooming season with a high-phosphorus fertilizer to maximize flowers. Prune in early spring to control size and shape. Pinch growing tips to encourage branching and more flowers.
🏴
Texas Tip
Mandevilla is a Texas porch favorite — the hot weather actually boosts flowering. Give it something to climb on the porch railing and it will reward you with non-stop red trumpets all summer long. In Texas, treat it as a tender perennial — bring indoors before the first frost. You can prune it back hard and overwinter it in a bright window. It will look bare in winter but flush back out in spring.
23
Moderate
Outdoor
Hibiscus rosa-sinensis

Hibiscus

A young tropical hibiscus establishing in the garden bed, showing vigorous fresh green growth with characteristic serrated, glossy leaves. Once mature, it will produce large, showy trumpet-shaped flowers in vibrant colors. Hibiscus is the quintessential tropical garden plant, beloved for its spectacular blooms that can reach 4–6 inches across.

💧
Watering
Consistent, regular moisture
Keep soil consistently moist during the growing season, especially while young and establishing. Water deeply 2–3 times per week; daily in extreme Texas heat. Hibiscus does not tolerate drought well — dry soil causes bud drop and leaf yellowing. Mulch around the base to conserve moisture.
☀️
Light
Full sun — 6+ hours
Hibiscus needs at least 6 hours of direct sunlight for prolific flowering. More sun means more blooms. It can tolerate some afternoon shade in extreme Texas heat, but too much shade significantly reduces flowering.
🌡️
Temperature
60–95 °F for active growth
Tropical hibiscus loves Texas heat and blooms most in warm weather. Hardy hibiscus varieties (H. moscheutos) survive cold winters, but tropical hibiscus (H. rosa-sinensis) needs protection below 40 °F. Mulch heavily in fall or bring containers indoors. In mild Texas winters (Zone 9), it may survive outdoors with protection.
🌱
Extra Care
Feed heavily, watch for pests
Hibiscus is a heavy feeder — use a hibiscus-specific fertilizer (high in potassium) every 2 weeks during the growing season for maximum blooms. Prune in early spring to shape and encourage branching. Watch for aphids, whiteflies, and spider mites, which are common in Texas. Deadhead spent flowers to keep the plant tidy.
🏴
Texas Tip
Tropical hibiscus is a showstopper in Texas gardens. Once your young plant establishes, expect large, colorful blooms from late spring through fall. Keep it well-watered through this first Texas summer to build strong roots. The biggest pest challenges in Texas are whiteflies and aphids — inspect the undersides of leaves regularly and treat with insecticidal soap or neem oil. Feed every 2 weeks with a high-potassium fertilizer for the most spectacular flowers. If you get a hard freeze warning, mulch heavily or cover with frost cloth.
24
Moderate
Outdoor
Mandevilla spp.

Dipladenia

A compact variety of Mandevilla (often sold as Dipladenia) planted in the ground near a garden border. It features bright red trumpet flowers with yellow throats and glossy, deep green oval leaves. This bush-type Mandevilla stays more compact than vining varieties, making it perfect for garden beds.

💧
Watering
Moderate, well-drained soil
Water regularly to keep soil evenly moist during the growing season. Once established, it's moderately drought-tolerant. Ensure good drainage — Dipladenias don't like wet feet. Water at the base to avoid leaf diseases.
☀️
Light
Full sun to partial shade
Performs best in full sun with at least 6 hours of direct light. Some afternoon shade in Texas can prevent flower fade. The garden bed location should provide plenty of light.
🌡️
Temperature
60–90 °F for blooming
Loves Texas heat and blooms most prolifically in warm weather. Not cold-hardy — the roots may survive mild Texas winters with heavy mulching, but the tops will die back in a freeze. In colder areas, treat as an annual or dig up the tuber.
🌱
Extra Care
Feed for continuous blooms
Apply a balanced slow-release fertilizer in spring and supplement with liquid fertilizer every 2–3 weeks during peak bloom. Deadhead spent flowers to encourage new blooms. Mulch around the base to conserve moisture and regulate soil temperature.
🏴
Texas Tip
Planted in the ground, your Dipladenia will love the Texas heat and bloom continuously through summer. The red flowers with yellow throats are hummingbird magnets. Mulch heavily around the base — in mild Texas winters (Zone 8b–9), the roots may survive with 3–4 inches of mulch. If we get a hard freeze, it may not come back, so take cuttings in fall as insurance. This is a great garden bed choice for non-stop summer color.
25
Moderate
Outdoor
Jasminum spp.

Jasmine

A young jasmine bush planted in the ground near the porch steps, showing fresh green growth with small, glossy oval leaves. Jasmine is beloved for its intensely fragrant white flowers that perfume the air, especially on warm evenings. Your plant is establishing well and will fill out with time.

💧
Watering
Regular, consistent moisture
Water deeply 2–3 times per week while establishing. Once rooted, jasmine is moderately drought-tolerant but blooms better with consistent moisture. Water at the base, keeping foliage dry to prevent fungal issues.
☀️
Light
Full sun to partial shade
Jasmine blooms best with at least 4–6 hours of sun. It can tolerate some shade but will produce fewer flowers. Morning sun with light afternoon shade is a good balance for Texas.
🌡️
Temperature
Hardy to 20 °F (depending on variety)
Many jasmine varieties are surprisingly cold-hardy for Texas. Confederate Jasmine (Trachelospermum) is evergreen and hardy to about 10 °F. True Jasmine (Jasminum) varies by species. Your plant should handle normal Texas winters with minimal protection.
🌱
Extra Care
Train and prune for shape
Jasmine can be trained as a shrub, vine, or ground cover depending on the variety. Prune after the main flush of flowers to shape. Feed in spring with a balanced fertilizer. As it matures, it will fill out and provide wonderful fragrance near your porch steps — perfect placement.
🏴
Texas Tip
Jasmine is a wonderful choice for planting near your porch steps — as it matures, you'll be greeted with incredible fragrance every time you walk by. In Texas, jasmine generally thrives once established. Water it well through this first summer to help roots develop. Most varieties bloom heavily in spring and may rebloom in fall. Confederate Jasmine is the most popular type in Texas and is nearly bulletproof once established.
26
Moderate
Outdoor
Begonia x tuberhybrida

Begonia

A newly planted tuberous begonia in the garden bed, just starting to show its first pink bloom. The plant tag confirms it's an annual begonia variety. These begonias produce large, showy flowers in vibrant colors and are a favorite for adding color to shady garden spots.

💧
Watering
Even moisture, avoid overwatering
Keep soil consistently moist but never waterlogged. Begonias have tuberous roots that can rot in soggy soil. Water at the base, not on the leaves, as wet foliage promotes powdery mildew. Mulch to maintain even moisture.
☀️
Light
Partial shade to filtered sun
Tuberous begonias prefer shade or filtered light. A few hours of gentle morning sun is ideal, but avoid harsh afternoon sun, which will scorch the flowers and foliage. Plant under trees or on the north side of structures.
🌡️
Temperature
60–75 °F for best performance
Begonias prefer moderate temperatures and struggle in extreme heat. Texas summers above 95 °F can stress them. Choose the shadiest, coolest spot in the garden bed. They're not frost-hardy and will die with the first freeze.
🌱
Extra Care
Deadhead, feed lightly
Remove spent blooms to encourage continuous flowering. Feed every 2–3 weeks with a balanced liquid fertilizer. At the end of the season before frost, you can dig up the tubers, dry them, and store indoors over winter to replant next spring.
🏴
Texas Tip
Your newly planted begonia will add beautiful color to the garden bed. In Texas, the key challenge is heat — plant it in the shadiest part of the bed and mulch well to keep roots cool. Water in the morning so foliage dries before the humid evening. As an annual begonia, it won't survive winter, but it should bloom all the way until the first frost in November or December. The plant tag says annual, so enjoy it for this season.
27
Easy
Outdoor
Bougainvillea glabra

Bougainvillea

A vibrant bougainvillea bursting with brilliant magenta-pink papery bracts (the colorful parts are actually modified leaves, not petals). Planted in the ground near a stone pathway, this tropical showstopper is one of the most dramatic flowering plants you can grow. Yours is putting on a spectacular display.

💧
Watering
Low — prefers dry between waterings
Bougainvillea blooms best when slightly stressed by drought. Water deeply but infrequently, letting the soil dry out between waterings. Overwatering encourages leaf growth at the expense of flowers. Once established, it's remarkably drought-tolerant.
☀️
Light
Full sun — the more the better
Needs at least 6 hours of direct sun; 8+ is even better. Bougainvillea only blooms profusely in full sun. It's one of the few plants that actually thrives in the intense Texas sun.
🌡️
Temperature
65–100 °F, not frost-hardy
Loves heat — the hotter, the better for flowering. However, it's not cold-hardy. In-ground bougainvillea can survive mild Texas winters (Zone 9) with heavy mulching, but a hard freeze will kill it to the ground. In Zone 8, treat as a large annual or grow in a container to move indoors.
🌱
Extra Care
Prune for shape, stress for blooms
Prune after each bloom cycle to shape and encourage new growth (and new blooms). Feed with a low-nitrogen, high-phosphorus fertilizer to promote flowers over leaves. The thorny stems need heavy gloves for pruning. Can be trained on fences, arbors, or left as a sprawling bush.
🏴
Texas Tip
Bougainvillea is absolutely gorgeous in Texas — the intense sun and heat that stress most plants is exactly what bougainvillea craves. Your plant is putting on a fantastic show. The biggest risk is a hard winter freeze. Mulch heavily around the base in late fall with 4–6 inches of mulch. In a mild winter, it may come back from the roots even if the top freezes. If you're in Zone 8, consider having frost cloth ready for cold snaps below 30 °F.
28
Easy
Outdoor
Liriope muscari

Liriope

A clump-forming ornamental grass-like plant with long, narrow dark green leaves. Also called Monkey Grass or Lily Turf, Liriope is one of the most versatile and tough landscape plants available. Often used as ground cover or border edging, it produces purple flower spikes in late summer.

💧
Watering
Low once established
Water regularly during the first growing season to establish roots. Once established, Liriope is remarkably drought-tolerant and rarely needs supplemental water except during extended dry periods. Overwatering can cause root rot.
☀️
Light
Full sun to full shade
One of the most adaptable plants for light conditions. Grows in everything from full sun to deep shade, though flowering is best in sun to partial shade. Some browning of leaf tips in full Texas sun is normal.
🌡️
Temperature
Extremely hardy, -10 to 100+ °F
Nearly indestructible. Handles Texas heat, cold, humidity, and drought. Evergreen in most of Texas, though leaves may look ragged after a hard winter. One of the few plants that truly thrives year-round in Texas.
🌱
Extra Care
Mow back annually, divide as needed
In late February before new growth starts, mow or cut back the foliage to about 3 inches. Fresh new growth will emerge and the clump will look rejuvenated. Divide overgrown clumps every 3–4 years in spring. Feed once in spring with a general-purpose fertilizer.
🏴
Texas Tip
Liriope is one of the toughest plants for Texas landscapes — it handles heat, cold, drought, shade, and sun. Plant it and essentially forget about it. The only annual maintenance is cutting it back in late February before the new growth emerges. It will spread slowly to form an attractive ground cover. In late summer, watch for the purple flower spikes, which are followed by dark berries.
29
Moderate
Outdoor
Rhododendron spp.

Azalea

A beautiful azalea in full bloom, covered in vibrant hot pink flowers. Azaleas are iconic Southern garden plants, celebrated for their spectacular spring flower displays. Your potted specimen is putting on a gorgeous show with clusters of trumpet-shaped blooms covering the entire bush.

💧
Watering
Consistent moisture, acidic conditions
Azaleas have shallow root systems that dry out quickly. Keep soil consistently moist but well-drained. Mulch heavily with pine bark or pine needles (which also acidifies the soil). Water with rainwater when possible, as Texas tap water is often too alkaline.
☀️
Light
Partial shade — morning sun, afternoon shade
Ideal conditions are morning sun with afternoon dappled shade. Too much sun, especially hot Texas afternoon sun, scorches the leaves and fades blooms. Too much shade reduces flowering. Under a high-canopy tree is often perfect.
🌡️
Temperature
Hardy to about 0 °F
Most azalea varieties handle Texas winters easily. The challenge in Texas is actually the summer heat, not the cold. Provide afternoon shade and consistent water during summer heat waves.
🌱
Extra Care
Acidic soil is essential
Texas soil is naturally alkaline, which is the #1 challenge for azaleas. Use an acidic potting mix and feed with azalea-specific (acid-forming) fertilizer. Add sulfur to lower soil pH if needed. Prune immediately after blooming — never prune in fall or winter, as this removes next year's flower buds.
🏴
Texas Tip
Azaleas can be challenging in Texas because our soil and water are alkaline, while azaleas need acidic conditions (pH 5.0–6.0). Your potted setup is smart — you can control the soil mix. Use an azalea/camellia potting mix and fertilize with Holly-Tone or similar acid-forming fertilizer. Water with collected rainwater when possible. Mulch with pine bark. If leaves turn yellow between the veins (chlorosis), the pH is too high — add soil sulfur.
30
Moderate
Outdoor
Salvia spp.

Salvia

A vigorous salvia establishing in the garden bed, showing lush, textured foliage with the characteristic broad, coarsely serrated leaves of the sage family. Salvias are one of the most rewarding garden plants, producing tall spikes of tubular flowers that attract hummingbirds and butterflies. Yours is building a strong base of foliage that will support abundant flower spikes.

💧
Watering
Moderate — drought tolerant once established
Water regularly while establishing, then salvia becomes quite drought-tolerant. Water deeply once or twice a week rather than frequent light watering. Overwatering and poor drainage cause root rot. Once established, salvia handles dry Texas spells with ease.
☀️
Light
Full sun to light shade
Most salvias bloom best in full sun — at least 6 hours of direct light. Some varieties tolerate partial shade but will be leggier and bloom less. In Texas, full sun produces the most compact, floriferous plants.
🌡️
Temperature
Heat-loving, varies by species
Most salvias thrive in heat — many are native to Texas or similar warm climates. Perennial varieties like Salvia greggii (Autumn Sage) are hardy to about 10 °F and evergreen in mild Texas winters. Annual types like Salvia splendens die with the first frost but reseed easily.
🌱
Extra Care
Cut back for reblooming, attract pollinators
Deadhead or cut back spent flower spikes to encourage continuous reblooming throughout the season. Cut the entire plant back by one-third in late summer for a strong fall rebloom. Feed lightly in spring with a balanced fertilizer — salvias don't need heavy feeding. Excellent for attracting hummingbirds, butterflies, and bees to your garden.
🏴
Texas Tip
Salvia is one of the absolute best plants for Texas gardens — many species are native here and practically bulletproof once established. After it finishes its first flush of flowers, cut back the spent spikes and it will rebloom repeatedly through fall. The tubular flowers are hummingbird magnets; you'll see them visiting daily. Water regularly this first summer to establish deep roots, then it will need very little supplemental water. Mulch around the base to keep weeds down and retain some moisture during the hottest weeks.
31
Moderate
Outdoor
Codiaeum variegatum

Croton

A stunning tropical foliage plant with leathery leaves painted in bold combinations of red, orange, yellow, and green. Crotons are among the most colorful foliage plants available. Your new purchase shows beautiful autumn-toned variegation with deep reds and warm oranges throughout the leaves.

💧
Watering
Moderate, consistent moisture
Keep soil evenly moist but not waterlogged. Water when the top inch of soil feels dry. Crotons will drop leaves dramatically if they get too dry or too wet. Consistency is key. Reduce watering in winter.
☀️
Light
Full sun for best color
Crotons need bright light to maintain their vivid colors. In low light, new leaves emerge green and dull. At least 4–6 hours of direct sun brings out the reds, oranges, and yellows. Acclimate gradually to outdoor sun to prevent leaf burn.
🌡️
Temperature
60–85 °F, not frost-hardy
A true tropical that loves warmth. Thrives during Texas summer but must come indoors when temperatures drop below 50 °F. Crotons despise cold drafts and will drop leaves if exposed to chill.
🌱
Extra Care
Avoid moving, keep stable
Crotons are dramatic — they drop leaves when stressed by changes in location, watering, or temperature. Once you find a good spot, leave it there. Feed monthly during the growing season with a balanced liquid fertilizer. Wipe the large leaves to keep them glossy.
🏴
Texas Tip
Crotons are beautiful but sensitive to change — they'll drop leaves if moved too abruptly between indoors and outdoors. Acclimate gradually: a few hours outside the first day, increasing over a week. Once settled on the porch, they love Texas warmth and humidity. The vivid colors need sun, so give it a bright spot. Bring inside well before the first cold front and place in the sunniest window you have. Expect some leaf drop during the transition.
32
Easy
Outdoor
Canna x generalis

Canna Lily

A tropical showstopper with bold, bronze-tinted banana-like leaves and vivid orange flowers held high on tall stems. Your 'Cannova Bronze Leaf Orange' variety combines dramatic dark foliage with fiery orange blooms — a striking combination. Cannas bring an instant tropical feel to any garden.

💧
Watering
Regular to heavy watering
Cannas are heavy drinkers that love moisture. Water deeply and frequently, especially during hot weather. They can even grow in standing water at pond edges. In garden beds, water 2–3 times per week. Mulch to retain moisture.
☀️
Light
Full sun — 6–8 hours minimum
Cannas need full sun for the best flowering and leaf color. The bronze foliage of your variety gets its deepest color in strong sun. They can tolerate light shade but will be lankier and bloom less.
🌡️
Temperature
Thrives in heat, hardy to 0 °F (roots)
Cannas absolutely love Texas heat — they grow faster and bloom more as temperatures rise. The foliage dies back with the first frost, but the rhizomes (underground roots) are hardy to about 0 °F and will return in spring. In most of Texas, they're reliably perennial.
🌱
Extra Care
Plant deep, divide yearly
Plant the rhizome 4–6 inches deep. Cut back dead foliage after frost. In spring, divide overcrowded clumps every 2–3 years to maintain vigor. Feed monthly with a balanced fertilizer during the growing season. Remove spent flower stalks to encourage continued blooming.
🏴
Texas Tip
Canna Lilies are absolutely made for Texas — they thrive in the heat and grow like weeds during summer. Plant your 'Cannova Bronze Leaf Orange' in the sunniest spot with rich soil. Once planted, it will come back year after year, getting bigger and more impressive each season. The rhizomes survive Texas winters in the ground — just leave the dead foliage as insulation until late February, then cut back for fresh spring growth. Divide every few years to share with friends.
33
Easy
Outdoor
Lilium asiaticum

Asiatic Lily

A gorgeous bulb plant producing large, upward-facing star-shaped flowers in warm sunset shades of copper, pink, and orange. Asiatic Lilies are among the easiest lilies to grow, blooming in early to mid-summer. Your plant shows multiple blooms and buds, promising a continued show.

💧
Watering
Moderate, well-drained soil
Water regularly to keep soil evenly moist during growth and blooming. Good drainage is essential — lily bulbs will rot in soggy soil. Reduce watering after blooming as the foliage dies back naturally. Mulch to conserve moisture and keep roots cool.
☀️
Light
Full sun to light shade
Best with at least 6 hours of sun for strong stems and vibrant blooms. Partial afternoon shade in Texas can help flowers last longer by preventing them from fading in intense heat. A trick: 'head in the sun, feet in the shade' — mulch keeps roots cool.
🌡️
Temperature
Hardy, survives Texas winters
Asiatic Lily bulbs are cold-hardy and survive year-round in the ground across all of Texas. The foliage dies back after blooming and returns the following spring. Summer heat is fine as long as the soil doesn't dry out completely.
🌱
Extra Care
Plant bulbs deep, feed annually
Plant bulbs 6–8 inches deep in fall or early spring. After flowers fade, leave the foliage until it yellows naturally — it feeds the bulb for next year's blooms. Remove spent flowers to prevent seed formation (saves energy for the bulb). Top-dress with compost in fall.
🏴
Texas Tip
Asiatic Lilies do well in Texas — plant the bulbs in fall for the best spring establishment. Yours will bloom each summer with minimal care. After the flowers fade, resist cutting the foliage — it needs to photosynthesize to recharge the bulb for next year. The bulbs multiply over time, giving you more stems each year. In Texas, the main challenge is keeping soil cool in summer — a 3-inch mulch layer is essential. They also make excellent cut flowers.
34
Easy
Outdoor
Schefflera arboricola 'Trinette'

Variegated Schefflera

A cheerful Umbrella Plant with distinctive variegated foliage — glossy green leaves splashed with bright cream and yellow. Each leaf is composed of multiple leaflets arranged like an umbrella or a hand. The 'Trinette' variety is particularly prized for its vivid variegation.

💧
Watering
Moderate, allow top to dry
Water when the top 1–2 inches of soil dry out. Schefflera is quite forgiving and tolerates occasional missed waterings. Yellowing and dropping lower leaves is a sign of overwatering. Ensure the pot has good drainage.
☀️
Light
Bright indirect to partial sun
Needs bright light to maintain the vivid cream and yellow variegation — in low light, new growth may revert to solid green. Some direct morning sun is beneficial, but avoid harsh afternoon sun which can scorch the variegated portions of leaves.
🌡️
Temperature
60–80 °F ideal
Tropical plant that loves warmth. Bring indoors when temperatures drop below 50 °F. Thrives on a sheltered porch during Texas warm months. Avoid cold drafts.
🌱
Extra Care
Prune for shape, clean leaves
Schefflera responds well to pruning — cut back leggy growth to encourage bushier shape. Pinch growing tips to promote branching. Wipe leaves occasionally to keep them shiny. Feed monthly during the growing season. Watch for spider mites and scale insects.
🏴
Texas Tip
Your Variegated Schefflera will do great on a Texas porch during the warm months. The variegation needs bright light to stay vivid, so place it where it gets good morning light but is shaded from intense afternoon sun. Bring it indoors before temperatures drop below 50 °F. These plants can grow quite large over time — prune to control size and shape. In Texas humidity, watch for mealybugs and scale insects, especially on the undersides of leaves.
🌱
